title: 第 7 節
en: 'Some scholars/ he continues, 'more judicious, better instructed also in the history of the early nations whose colonies first discovered the East, and familiar with Oriental languages, have at last, after clearing mythology of the rubbish with which the Greeks had covered it, found the true key of it in the actual history of the early nations, their opinions and their rulers, in the false translations of a number of simple expressions, the meaning of which had been forgotten by those who nevertheless continued to use them ; and in the homonymies which out of one object, designated by various epithets, have made so many different beings or persons.

' But these keys which open so well the meaning of historical fables, do not always suffice to give a reason for the singularity of the dogmatic opinions, nor of the practical rites of the early nations. These two portions of heathen theology depend either 011 the worship of the celestial bodies, well known by the name of Sabeism, or on the probably not less ancient worship of certain terrestrial and material objects, called fetiche, by the African negroes (he meant to say by those who visited the African negroes), and which for this reason I shall call fetichisme. I ask permission to use this term habitually, and though in the proper signification it refers in particular to the religion of the negroes of Africa only, I give notice beforehand that I mean to use it with reference also to any other nation IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION ? 59 paying worship to animals, or to inanimate things which are changed into gods, even when these objects are less gods, in the proper sense of the word, than things endowed with a certain divine virtue, such as oracles, amulets, or protecting talis mans. For it is certain that all these forms of thought have one and the same origin, which belongs to one general religion, formerly spread over the whole earth, which must be examined by itself, constituting, as it does, a separate class among the various religions of the heathen world/ De Brosses divides his book into three parts. In the first he collects all the information which was then accessible on fetishism, as still practised by barbarous tribes in Africa and other parts of the world. In the second he compares it with the religious practices of the principal nations of anti quity. In the third he tries to show that, as these practices are very like to one another in their outward appearance, we may conclude that their original intention among the negroes of to-day and among the Egyptians, the Greeks, and Romans, was the same.
All nations, he holds, had to begin with fetishism, to be followed afterwards by polytheism and mono theism.

One nation only forms with him an exception— the Jews, the chosen people of God. They, accord ing to De Brosses, were never fetish-worshippers, while ah1 other nations first received a primeval divine revelation, then forgot it, and then began again from the beginning — viz. with fetishism.
It is curious to observe the influence which the prevalent theological ideas of the time exercised 60 LECTURE II.

even on De Brosses. If he had dared to look for traces of fetishism in the Old Testament with the same keenness which made him see fetishes in Egypt, in Greece, in Home, and everywhere else, surely the Teraphim, the Urim and Thummim, or the ephod, to say nothing of golden calves and brazen serpents, might have supplied him with ample material (Gen. xxviii. 18 ; Jerem. ii. 27).

But though on this and some other points those who have more recently adopted and defended the theory of De Brosses would differ from him, on the whole his view of fetishism has been maintained intact during the last hundred years. It sounded so easy, so natural, so plausible, that it soon found its way into manuals and schoolbooks, and I believe we all of us have been brought up on it1. I myself certainly held it for a long time, and never doubted it, till I became more and more startled by the fact that, while in the earliest accessible documents of religious thought we look in vain for any very clear traces of fetishism, they become more and more frequent everywhere in the later stages of religious development, and are certainly more visible in the later corruptions of the Indian religion2, beginning with the Atharvana, than in the earliest hymns of the Rig- Veda.

1 Meiners, whose ' Allgemeine Kritische Greschiclite der Religionen,' 1806, was for many years the chief storehouse for all who wrote on the history of religion, says : ' It cannot be denied that fetishism is not only the oldest, but also the most universal worship of gods.'

2 'L'etranger qui arrive dans 1'Inde, et moi-meme je n'ai pas fait exception a cette regie, ne decouvre d'abord que des pratiques religieuses aussi degradantes que degraddes, un vrai polytheisme, IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION 1 61 Origin of the name of fetish.

Why did the Portuguese navigators, who were Christians, but Christians in that metamorphic state which marks the popular Roman Catholicism of the last century — why did they recognise at once what they saw among the negroes of the Gold Coast, as feitifos ? The answer is clear. Because they them selves were perfectly familiar with a feitipo, an amulet or a talisman ; and probably all carried with them some beads, or crosses, or images, that had been blessed by their priests before they started for their voyage. They themselves were fetish-worship pers in a certain sense. What was more natural therefore for them, if they saw a native hugging some ornament, or unwilling to part with some glittering stone, or it may be prostrating himself and praying to some bones, carefully preserved in his hut, than to suppose that the negroes did not only keep these things for luck, but that they were sacred relics, something in fact like what they themselves would call feitifo ? As they discovered no other traces of any religious worship, they concluded very naturally that this outward show of regard for these feitipos constituted the whole of the negro's religion.

Suppose these negroes, after watching the pro ceedings of their white visitors, had asked on their part what the religion of those white men might be, what would they have said 1 They saw the Portu guese sailors handling their rosaries, burning incense to dauby images, bowing before altars, carrying gaudy presque du feticliisme.' — ' De la superiorite du Brahmanisme sur le Catliolicisme/ Conference donnee par M. Goblet d'Alviella.

flags, prostrating themselves before a wooden cross. They did not see them while saying their prayers, they never witnessed any sacrifices offered by them to their gods, nor was their moral conduct such as to give the natives the idea that they abstained from any crimes, because they feared the gods. What would have been more natural therefore for them than to say that their religion seemed to consist in a worship of gru-grus, their own name for what the Portuguese called feitifo, and that they had no idea of a supreme spirit or a king of heaven, or offered any worship to him \ With regard to the word, it is well known that the Portuguese feitifo corresponds to Latin factitius. Factitius, from meaning what is made by hand, came to mean artificial, then unnatural, magical, enchanted and enchanting. A false key is called in Portuguese chave feitifa, while feitipo becomes the recognised name for amulets and similar half-sacred trinkets. The trade in such articles was perfectly recognised in Europe during the middle ages, as it is still among the negroes of Africa. A manufacturer or seller of them was called fetiif&ro, a word which, however, was likewise used in the sense of a magician or con jurer. How common the word was in Portuguese we see from its being used in its diminutive form as a term of endearment, men feitipinho meaning my little fetish, or darling.

We see a similar transition of meaning in the Sanskrit ki'ityd, the Italian fattura, incantation, which occurs in mediseval Latin as far back as 1 3 1 1 * ; also 1 ' Synodus Pergam.,' arm. 1311, apud Muratorium, torn. 9, col. 561 ; incantationes, sacrilegia, auguria, vel rnaleficia, quae facturae sive prsestigia vulgariter appellantur.
IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION? 63 in charme, which was originally no more than car men ; and in the Greek eV<w<5j/.
Wrong extension of the name fetish.

It will be clear from these considerations that the Portuguese sailors — for it is to them that we are indebted for the introduction of the word fetish — could have applied that term to certain tangible and inanimate objects only, and that it was an un warrantable liberty taken with the word which enabled De Brosses to extend it to animals, and to such things as mountains, trees, and livers. De O '

Brosses imagined that the name f&tiipo was some how related tofatum, and its modern derivative fata (nom. plur. of the neuter, used afterwards as a nom. sing, of the feminine), a fee, a fairy ; and this may have made it appear less incongruous to him to apply the name of fetish, not only to artificial and material objects, but also to trees, mountains, rivers, and even to animals. This was the first unfortunate step on the part of De Brosses, for he thus mixed up three totally distinct phases of religion, first, physiolatry, or the worship paid to natural objects which impress the mind of man with feelings of awe or gratitude, such as rivers, trees, or mountains ; secondly, zoolatry, or the worship paid to animals, as for instance by the highly-cultivated inhabitants of ancient Egypt ; and lastly, fetishism proper, or the superstitious venera tion felt and testified for mere rubbish, apparently without any claim to such distinction.

But this was not all. De Brosses did not keep what he calls fetish-worship distinct even from idol atry, though there is a very important distinction be tween the two. A fetish, properly so called, is itself 64 LECTURE II.

regarded as something supernatural ; the idol, on the contrary, was originally meant as an image only, a similitude or a symbol of something else. No doubt an idol was apt to become a fetish ; but in the begin ning, fetish worship, in the proper sense of the word, springs from a source totally different from that which produces idolatry.

Let us hear how De Brosses explains his idea of a fetish. ' These fetishes,' he says, ' are anything which people like to select for adoration, — a tree, a mountain, the sea, a piece of wood, the tail of a lion, a pebble, a shell, salt, a fish, a plant, a flower, certain animals, such as cows, goats, elephants, sheep, or anything like these. These are the gods of the negro, sacred objects, talismans. The negroes offer them worship, address their prayers to them, perform sacri fices, carry them about in procession, consult them on great occasions. They swear by them, and such oaths are never broken.

' There are fetishes belonging to a whole tribe, and others belonging to individuals. National fetishes have a kind of public sanctuary ; private fetishes are kept in their own place in the houses of private individuals.

' If the negroes want rain, they place an empty jar before the fetish. When they go to battle, they deposit their weapons before it or him. If they are in want of fish or meat, bare bones are laid down before the fetish ; while, if they wish for palm-wine, they indicate their desire by leaving with the fetish the scissors with which the incisions are made in the palm-trees 1. If their prayers are heard, all is right.

1 Similar customs mentioned by "Waitz, ' Anthropologie,' vol. ii. P-I77- IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION ? 65 But if they are refused, they think that they have somehow incurred the anger of their fetish, and they try to appease him.'

Such is a short abstract of what De Brosses meant by fetishism, what he believed the religions of the negroes to be, and what he thought the religion of all the great nations of antiquity must have been before they reached the higher stages of polytheism and monotheism.
Usefulness of the study of savage tribes.

The idea that, in order to understand what the so-called civilised people may have been before they reached their higher enlightenment, we ought to study savage tribes, such as we find them still at the present day, is perfectly just. It is the lesson which geology has taught us, applied to the stratification of the human race. But the danger of mistaking metamorphic for primary igneous rocks is much less in geology than in anthropology. Allow me to quote some excellent remarks on this point by Mr. Herbert Spencer1. 'To determine/ he writes, 'what con ceptions are truly primitive, would be easy if we had accounts of truly primitive men. But there are sundry reasons for suspecting that existing men of the lowest types, forming social groups of the simplest kinds, do not exemplify men as they origi nally were. Probably most of them, if not all, had ancestors in higher states ; and among their beliefs remain some which were evolved during those higher 1 'Sociology/ p. 1 06. See also 'On some Characteristics of Malayo-Poiynesians/ in ' Journal of the Anthropological Institute/ February, 1878.

states. While the degradation theory, as currently held, is untenable, the theory of progression, taken in its unqualified form, seems to me untenable also. If, on the one hand, the notion that savagery is caused by lapse from civilisation is irreconcilable with the evidence, there is, on the other hand, inadequate warrant for the notion that the lowest savagery has always been as low as it is now. It is quite possible , and, I believe, highly probable, that retrogression has been as frequent as progression/ These words contain a most useful warning for those ethnologists who imagine that they have only to spend a few years among Papuas, Fuegians, or Andaman Islanders, in order to know what the primitive ancestors of the Greeks and Kornans may have been. They speak of the savage of to-day as if he had only just been sent into the world, forgetting that, as a living species, he is probably not a day younger than we ourselves1. He may be a more stationary being, but he may also have passed through many ups and downs before he reached his present level. Anyhow, even if it could be proved that there has been a con tinuous progression in everything else, no one could maintain that the same applies to religion.
Frequent retrogression in Beligion.

That religion is liable to corruption is surely seen again and again in the history of the world. In one sense the history of most religions might be called a 1 'The savage are as old as the civilised races, and can as little be named primitive.' — A. M. Fairbairn, ' Academy,' 20 July, IS FETISHISM A PKIMITIVE FOEM OF RELIGION? 67 slow corruption of their primitive purity. At all events, no one would venture to maintain that religion always keeps pace with general civilisation. Even admitting therefore that, with regard to their tools, their dress, their manners and customs, the Greeks and Romans, the Germans and Celts may have been before the first dawn of history in the same state in which we find some of the negro races of Africa at present, nothing would justify the con clusion that their religion also must have been the same, that they must have worshipped fetishes, stocks and stones, and nothing else.

We see Abraham, a mere nomad, fully impressed with the necessity of the unity of the godhead, while Solomon, famous among the kings of the earth, built high places for Chemosh and Moloch. Ephesus, in the sixth century before Christ, was listening to one of the wisest men that Greece ever produced, Herakleitos ; while a thousand years later, the same town resounded with the frivolous and futile wranglings of Cyril] us, and the council of Ephesus. The Hindus, who, thousands of years ago, had reached in the Upanishads the loftiest heights of philosophy, are now in many places sunk into a grovelling worship of cows and monkeys.
zh: > 他繼續說道：「有些學者，更為明智，也更熟悉那些最早發現東方的殖民者所屬的早期民族的歷史，且通曉東方語言，終於在清除了希臘人覆蓋於神話之上的廢料之後，找到了真正的鑰匙——這鑰匙存在於早期民族的實際歷史、他們的意見與統治者之中；存在於大量簡單表達的錯誤翻譯之中——這些表達的意義雖已被繼續使用它們的人所遺忘；也存在於同音異義之中——這些同音異義從一個以各種別名指稱的對象，創造出如此眾多不同的存在者或人物。」

「這些鑰匙雖能順利開啟歷史寓言的意義，卻未必總能解釋早期民族教義觀點的獨特性，也無法充分說明其實踐儀式的特殊性。異教神學的這兩個部分，若非源於對天體的崇拜（即眾所周知的薩比教），便是源於對某些陸地與物質對象的崇拜——這種崇拜可能同樣古老，被非洲黑人（他意指那些造訪非洲黑人的探險者）稱為『物神』，因此我將稱之為『物神崇拜』。我請求準許慣常使用此術語；雖然就其恰當含義而言，此詞特指非洲黑人的宗教，但我事先聲明，我打算將其用於任何其他民族——**物神崇拜是宗教的原始形式嗎？**——只要該民族崇拜動物、或崇拜被轉化為神靈的無生命物體，即使這些對象在嚴格意義上與其說是神靈，不如說是具有某種神聖力量的物品，例如神諭、護身符或保護性符咒。因為可以肯定，所有這些思想形式都源於同一個起源，屬於一種普遍宗教；這種宗教曾遍佈全球，必須單獨加以考察，因為它在異教世界的各種宗教中構成一個獨立的類別。」德布羅斯將其著作分為三部分。第一部分，他收集了當時所能取得的所有關於物神崇拜的資料——這種崇拜至今仍在非洲及世界其他地區的野蠻部落中實行。第二部分，他將物神崇拜與古代主要民族的宗教實踐進行比較。第三部分，他試圖證明：由於這些實踐在外觀上極為相似，我們可以推斷，今日的黑人與當年的埃及人、希臘人、羅馬人，其原始意圖是相同的。
他認為，所有民族都必須從拜物教（fetishism）開始，隨後才進入多神教（polytheism）與一神教（monotheism）。
對他而言，只有一個民族構成例外——猶太人，上帝的選民。根據德布羅斯（De Brosses）的說法，他們從未成為拜物教（fetish-worship）的信徒；而所有其他民族先是接受了原始的聖神啟示，隨後遺忘了它，接著又從頭開始——亦即從拜物教（fetishism）起步。
觀察當時盛行的神學觀念所施加的影響，是件耐人尋味的事。
即使是對德‧布羅斯（De Brosses）而言，倘若他敢以同樣的敏銳眼光——那種使他在埃及、希臘、羅馬及其他各地皆看出物神崇拜的銳利——去《舊約》中尋找物神崇拜的痕跡，那麼，更不用說金牛犢與銅蛇了，光是家神像（Teraphim）、烏陵與土明（Urim and Thummim），或以弗得（ephod），便足以為他提供充分的材料（創世記廿八章18節；耶利米書二章27節）。

但儘管在這一點及其他若干觀點上，後來採納並捍衛德布羅斯理論的人會與他有所分歧，整體而言，他對物神崇拜的看法在過去一百年間仍保持不變。這套理論聽起來如此簡明、如此自然、如此合理，以至於很快就滲入各類手冊與教科書，我相信我們所有人都是受此薰陶長大的¹。我本人也曾長期堅信此說，從未懷疑，直到我愈來愈震驚地發現：在最早可及的宗教思想文獻中，我們幾乎找不到任何清晰的物神崇拜痕跡；然而在宗教發展的後期階段，這些痕跡卻在各處變得愈發頻繁，尤其在印度宗教後期的墮落形態中——從《阿闥婆吠陀》開始——遠比《梨俱吠陀》最早的讚歌中更為顯著²。
1 邁納斯（Meiners）在其 1806 年出版的《宗教通史批判》（Allgemeine Kritische Geschichte der Religionen）中——該書多年來一直是所有宗教史撰寫者的主要資料庫——說道：「不可否認，拜物教不僅是最古老的，也是最普遍的神靈崇拜。」
2 「初抵印度之異鄉人——我本人亦未能倖免於此——最初所見，無非是既墮落又備受貶抑的宗教實踐，一種真正的多神教。**拜物教是否為宗教的原始形式** 1 61 拜物一名之起源。」

為何那些葡萄牙航海家——他們是基督徒，但卻處於一種變形狀態的基督徒，這種狀態標誌著上個世紀流行的羅馬天主教——為何他們能立刻認出在黃金海岸的黑人當中所見之物為**feitifos**（護符）？答案很清楚。因為他們自己對**feitipo**（一種護符或辟邪物）極為熟悉；而且很可能所有人在啟航前，都隨身攜帶了一些經由祭司祝福過的珠子、十字架或聖像。他們自己在某種意義上就是拜物者。因此，當他們看到一個土著緊抱著某件飾物、不願捨棄某塊閃亮的石頭，或者可能正對著小心保存在茅屋中的某些骨骸俯伏祈禱時，對他們而言，最自然不過的推測，不就是認為那些黑人不但把這些東西當作好運符，而且它們還是聖物，實際上就像他們自己所稱的**feitifo**一樣嗎？由於他們沒有發現任何其他宗教崇拜的痕跡，便很自然地得出結論：這種對這些**feitifos**的外在敬奉表現，構成了黑人宗教的全部。
假設這些黑人，在觀察了那些白人訪客的行為之後，反過來問那些白人的宗教究竟是什麼，他們又會如何回答呢？他們看到葡萄牙水手撥弄著念珠，向粗糙的圖像焚香，在祭壇前鞠躬，並攜帶華麗的聖物——幾乎可說是「近乎拜物教」。——〈論婆羅門教優於天主教〉，戈布萊‧達爾維耶拉先生講座。

他們向著木製十字架俯伏跪拜，卻從未見過他們在祈禱時注視這些旗幟，也從未目睹他們向自己的神祇獻祭；而他們的道德行為，更不足以讓當地人認為他們因畏懼神靈而戒絕任何罪行。因此，對當地人而言，最自然的推論莫過於：他們的宗教似乎就在於崇拜一種「gru-grus」（這是當地人對葡萄牙人所謂「feitifo」的稱呼），而且他們對至高之靈或天國之王毫無概念，也從未向祂獻上任何敬拜。 關於這個詞，眾所周知，葡萄牙語的「feitifo」對應於拉丁語的「factitius」。「Factitius」原意為「手工製作的」，後來引申為「人造的」，進而演變為「不自然的」、「魔法的」、「被施咒的」以及「迷人的」。在葡萄牙語中，一把假鑰匙被稱為「chave feitifa」，而「feitipo」則成為護身符及類似半神聖小飾品的公認名稱。在中世紀的歐洲，這類物品的交易完全被認可，正如它在今日非洲黑人之間仍然存在一樣。製造或販賣這些物品的人被稱為「fetiif&ro」，這個詞同樣也被用來指稱魔術師或變戲法的人。這個詞在葡萄牙語中多麼常見，我們可以從其指小形式被用作愛稱這一點看出：「men feitipinho」意即「我的小護身符」或「親愛的」。

我們在梵文的 *ki'ityd*、義大利文的 *fattura*（意為「咒語」）中，看到類似的意義轉變，該詞在中世紀拉丁文中最早可追溯至 1311 年*；另見《佩加蒙會議》（*Synodus Pergam.*），1311 年，收錄於穆拉托里（Muratorium）著作，第 9 卷，第 561 欄；其中提及「咒語、褻瀆、占卜或惡行，這些在俗語中被稱為 *facturae* 或 *præstigia*。」
在魅力上，原本不過是「凡人」；而在希臘文 *eV<w<5j/* 中。
從這些考量可以清楚看出，葡萄牙水手——因為正是他們為我們引入了「物神」（fetish）一詞——當初只能將該詞應用於某些可觸及的無生命物體；而德‧布羅斯（De Brosses）擅自擴張該詞的用法，將其延伸至動物以及山嶽、樹木與肝臟這類事物，實屬一種不當的任意之舉。

布羅塞曾設想，fétiço 這個名稱與 fatum 及其現代衍生詞 fata（中性名詞複數主格，後用作陰性名詞單數主格，意為「仙女」或「精靈」）有某種關聯；這或許使他覺得，將「物神」（fetish）一名不僅用於人造與物質的物件，也用於樹木、山巒、河流，甚至動物，似乎不那麼格格不入。這是德‧布羅塞踏出的第一個不幸步驟，因為他因此混淆了宗教的三個截然不同的階段：第一，自然崇拜（physiolatry），亦即對那些以敬畏或感恩之情打動人心的自然物（如河流、樹木或山巒）所行的禮拜；第二，動物崇拜（zoolatry），例如古埃及高度文明的居民所行的動物禮拜；最後，狹義的物神崇拜（fetishism proper），亦即對純粹廢棄之物所懷抱並表現出的迷信崇敬，而這些物件顯然毫無資格享有這等殊榮。
但這還不是全部。德‧布羅斯並未將他所謂的「物神崇拜」與偶像崇拜明確區分開來，儘管兩者之間存在著非常重要的區別。嚴格來說，所謂的物神，其本身即是……（第六十四頁，第二講）。
被視為某種超自然之物；反之，偶像（idol）原本僅被當作一種形象、某種他物的相似物或象徵。無疑地，偶像容易變成物神（fetish）；然而在初始階段，就該詞的嚴格意義而言，物神崇拜（fetish worship）的源頭與產生偶像崇拜（idolatry）的源頭全然不同。

讓我們聽聽德布羅斯如何解釋他對物神（fetish）的看法。他說：「這些物神（fetishes）是人們樂於選來崇拜的任何東西——一棵樹、一座山、海洋、一塊木頭、獅子的尾巴、一顆卵石、一個貝殼、鹽、一條魚、一株植物、一朵花、某些動物，例如牛、山羊、大象、綿羊，或諸如此類之物。這些就是黑人的神靈、聖物、護身符。黑人向它們獻上崇拜，對它們祈禱，舉行獻祭，列隊抬著它們遊行，在重大場合向它們求問。他們憑它們起誓，而這類誓言從不違背。」
> 有些物神屬於整個部落，另一些則屬於個人。民族的物神擁有一種公共聖所；私人的物神則保存在個人住處中屬於自己的位置。
' 如果黑人想要雨水，他們會在神靈（fetish）前放置一個空罐。當他們出征時，會將武器存放在它或祂面前。如果他們缺乏魚或肉，便將光骨頭擺在神靈前；而若他們想要棕櫚酒，則透過留下修剪棕櫚樹切口用的剪刀在神靈旁，來表明他們的願望¹。如果他們的祈禱應驗了，一切便安然無恙。
1 類似習俗見於「Waitz, *Anthropologie*，卷二，頁一七七。 **物神崇拜是否為宗教的原始形式？** 65 但若遭拒絕，他們便認為自己在某種程度上觸怒了物神，並試圖安撫祂。」
以下是根據您的要求，將弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒（Friedrich Max Müller）《宗教學導論》英文原文翻譯成的繁體中文： 這就是德‧布羅斯（De Brosses）所謂「物神崇拜」的簡要概述，他認為黑人的宗教即是如此，並且他相信，古代所有偉大民族的宗教，在達到多神教與一神教的更高階段之前，必然也曾經歷過這樣的形態。

為了理解所謂文明民族在達到更高啟蒙之前可能處於何種狀態，我們應當研究現今仍可找到的野蠻部落，這個想法完全正確。這是地質學教導我們的道理，應用於人類種族的分層。然而，在人類學中，將變質巖誤認為原生火成岩的危險，遠比在地質學中來得大。請允許我引用赫伯特‧斯賓塞先生對此問題的一些精闢評論¹。他寫道：「要確定哪些觀念真正原始，若我們擁有真正原始人類的記述，本該容易。但有多種理由令人懷疑，現存最低類型的人類——形成最簡單社會群體者——並非人類最初狀態的範例。他們之中，若非全部，也多半曾有處於更高狀態的祖先；而在他們的信仰中，仍保留著一些在那些更高狀態中演化出來的殘餘。」¹《社會學》，第106頁。另見〈論馬來-玻里尼西亞人的某些特徵〉，載於《人類學研究所期刊》，1878年2月。

states. 雖然目前所持的退化論（degradation theory）站不住腳，但未經限定的進化論（theory of progression），在我看來同樣難以成立。一方面，認為野蠻狀態源於文明墮落的觀點與證據不符；另一方面，認定最低等的野蠻狀態始終如現在這般低劣，也缺乏充分依據。完全有可能——而且我認為極有可能——退化與進化同樣頻繁。這些話對那些民族學家而言，是一項極具價值的告誡：他們以為只需在巴布亞人、火地島人或安達曼島人中待上幾年，就能瞭解希臘人與羅馬人的原始祖先可能是什麼模樣。他們談論今日的野蠻人，彷彿這群人剛被送入世界，卻忘了作為一個現存物種，他們很可能不比我們自己年輕一天¹。他們或許是更停滯不前的存在，但在達到目前水準之前，也可能經歷了許多起落。無論如何，即使能證明其他一切事物皆持續進步，也無人能主張宗教同樣適用此規律。

> 然而，若我們從宗教學的立場來審視這些事實，便會發現一個顯著的現象：在宗教的歷史進程中，不僅有進步，也有頻繁的倒退。我們看到，人類曾經達到較高的宗教觀念，卻又墮入較低的形態；曾經領悟到一神教（monotheism）的真理，卻又退回多神教（polytheism）或拜物教。這種倒退並非偶然，而是與語言、神話以及人類心智的運作方式密切相關。正如語言科學（the science of language）告訴我們，語言的發展並非直線前進，而是充滿了「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）所導致的扭曲與蛻變；同樣地，宗教的歷史也充滿了類似的病變與退化。

宗教易於墮落，在世界歷史中屢見不鮮。從某種意義上說，大多數宗教的歷史，都可稱為其原始純潔性緩慢墮落的過程。¹『野蠻人與文明種族同樣古老，絕不能稱為原始。』——A. M. 費爾貝恩，《學院》，7月20日，〈拜物教是宗教的原始形式嗎？〉第67頁。總之，無人敢斷言宗教始終與普遍文明同步發展。因此，即使承認希臘人、羅馬人、日耳曼人與凱爾特人在工具、衣著、風俗習慣上，於歷史曙光初現之前，可能處於與今日非洲某些黑人種族相同的狀態，也絕無理由斷定他們的宗教必然相同，斷定他們必定崇拜物神、木頭與石頭，而別無其他。
我們看見亞伯拉罕，一名單純的遊牧者，對神性合一之必要性深信不疑；而所羅門，這位地上萬王中最負盛名者，卻為基抹與摩洛建築邱壇。西元前六世紀的以弗所，正聆聽著希臘有史以來最睿智的哲人之一——赫拉克利特——的教誨；然而千年之後，同一座城鎮卻充斥著區利羅及其以弗所會議的輕浮而徒勞的爭辯。印度人，數千年前已在《奧義書》中達到哲學的至高巔峰，如今在許多地方卻墮入對牛與猴的卑屈崇拜。
